Benchmark Results for claude-haiku-4-5-LowReasoning

Test 1: Travelling Salesman Problem Solver (C++) Score: 2.50 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY number of cities from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 10-40 cities (brute-force feasible for smallest cases)
- **Medium**: 100-1000 cities (requires heuristics)
- **Large**: 5000-100000 cities (requires efficient heuristics)
- **Extreme**: 500000-5000000 cities (requires very fast approximations)
- **Epic**: 10000000+ cities (requires highly optimized approximations)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with city counts ranging from 10 to 10000000. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of cities)
Lines 2..N+1: x y coordinates (floats)

**Output format (stdout):**
One line with N integers: the visit order of city indices (0-indexed), starting at city 0.
Do NOT include returning to city 0 (return is implicit).

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for 1000,000,000 cities
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable routes at all scales
4. **Correctness**: Output must be a valid permutation of 0..N-1 starting with 0

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function that reads stdin and writes stdout.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on N.

Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

View chain of thought

Given a list of cities, find the shortest round-trip route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start. This is one of the most famous hard problems in computer science — checking every possible route is only feasible for a handful of cities, so the AI must use clever shortcuts to find a good route quickly.

Subpasses scale from 10 cities up to 1 000, so brute-force is impossible for the larger cases. The AI's route length is compared against a simple baseline; beating it earns full marks.

 1.0000
[10 cities] Route distance: 2481.89, Baseline: 2922.93, Ratio: 0.85x, Time: 0.4s - excellent (within 10% of baseline)

TSP Solver - 10 Cities

View Code (4445 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric>
#include <limits>
#include <cstring>

using namespace std;

struct City {
    double x, y;
    int id;
};

inline double dist_sq(const City& a, const City& b) {
    double dx = a.x - b.x;
    double dy = a.y - b.y;
    return dx * dx + dy * dy;
}

// Nearest neighbor for small-medium problems
vector<int> nearestNeighbor(const vector<City>& cities) {
    int n = cities.size();
    vector<bool> visited(n, false);
    vector<int> tour;
    tour.push_back(0);
    visited[0] = true;

    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        int last = tour.back();
        int nearest = -1;
        double minDist = numeric_limits<double>::max();

        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            if (!visited[j]) {
                double d = dist_sq(cities[last], cities[j]);
                if (d < minDist) {
                    minDist = d;
                    nearest = j;
                }
            }
        }

        tour.push_back(nearest);
        visited[nearest] = true;
    }

    return tour;
}

// Grid-based approach for large problems
vector<int> gridBasedTour(const vector<City>& cities) {
    int n = cities.size();

    // Find bounds
    double minX = cities[0].x, maxX = cities[0].x;
    double minY = cities[0].y, maxY = cities[0].y;
    for (const auto& c : cities) {
        minX = min(minX, c.x);
        maxX = max(maxX, c.x);
        minY = min(minY, c.y);
        maxY = max(maxY, c.y);
    }

    // Adaptive grid size
    int gridSize = max(1, (int)sqrt(n / 100));
    double cellWidth = (maxX - minX + 1e-9) / gridSize;
    double cellHeight = (maxY - minY + 1e-9) / gridSize;

    // Assign cities to grid cells
    vector<pair<pair<int, int>, int>> gridCities;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        int gx = (int)((cities[i].x - minX) / cellWidth);
        int gy = (int)((cities[i].y - minY) / cellHeight);
        gx = min(gx, gridSize - 1);
        gy = min(gy, gridSize - 1);
        gridCities.push_back({{gx * gridSize + gy, i}, i});
    }

    // Sort by grid cell (space-filling order)
    sort(gridCities.begin(), gridCities.end());

    // Extract sorted indices
    vector<int> tour;
    tour.reserve(n);
    for (const auto& p : gridCities) {
        tour.push_back(p.second);
    }

    return tour;
}

// Simple 2-opt for small problems only
void twoOpt(vector<int>& tour, const vector<City>& cities, int maxIter) {
    int n = tour.size();
    bool improved = true;
    int iter = 0;

    while (improved && iter < maxIter) {
        improved = false;
        iter++;

        for (int i = 1; i < n - 2; i++) {
            for (int j = i + 2; j < n; j++) {
                double delta =
                    dist_sq(cities[tour[i-1]], cities[tour[j]]) +
                    dist_sq(cities[tour[i]], cities[tour[(j+1)%n]]) -
                    dist_sq(cities[tour[i-1]], cities[tour[i]]) -
                    dist_sq(cities[tour[j]], cities[tour[(j+1)%n]]);

                if (delta < -1e-9) {
                    reverse(tour.begin() + i, tour.begin() + j + 1);
                    improved = true;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n;
    cin >> n;

    vector<City> cities(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> cities[i].x >> cities[i].y;
        cities[i].id = i;
    }

    vector<int> tour;

    if (n <= 100) {
        // Trivial: nearest neighbor + aggressive 2-opt
        tour = nearestNeighbor(cities);
        twoOpt(tour, cities, n * n);
    } else if (n <= 5000) {
        // Small-Medium: nearest neighbor + limited 2-opt
        tour = nearestNeighbor(cities);
        twoOpt(tour, cities, min(n, 1000));
    } else if (n <= 50000) {
        // Medium-Large: nearest neighbor only
        tour = nearestNeighbor(cities);
    } else {
        // Large-Extreme-Epic: grid-based fast approximation
        tour = gridBasedTour(cities);
    }

    // Verify tour starts with 0
    if (tour[0] != 0) {
        int pos = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (tour[i] == 0) {
                pos = i;
                break;
            }
        }
        rotate(tour.begin(), tour.begin() + pos, tour.end());
    }

    // Output
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (i > 0) cout << " ";
        cout << tour[i];
    }
    cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}

Test 2: Chinese Postman Problem Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection Problem) in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-20 nodes, 8-45 edges (small graphs, exact algorithms feasible)
- **Medium**: 35-200 nodes, 80-600 edges (requires efficient algorithms)
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, 2000-5000 edges (requires optimized implementations)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, 30000-80000 edges (requires very fast algorithms)
- **Epic**: 50000-100000 nodes, 500000-1000000 edges (requires highly optimized algorithms)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 100000 nodes. The same function must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v w (edge endpoints, 0-indexed, weight integer)

**Output format (stdout):**
A sequence of node indices (whitespace-separated) representing a route that starts at node 0,
traverses EVERY edge at least once, and returns to node 0.
You may optionally prefix with a single integer L (route length). If present, it must match the number of nodes listed.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Correctness**: Must traverse every edge at least once

**Environment:**

Architecture: AMD64

Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB

Imagine a mail carrier who must walk along every street in a neighbourhood and return home. Some streets may need to be walked twice if the network doesn't allow a perfect loop — the goal is to minimise that extra walking.

The AI must figure out which streets (edges) to re-traverse and produce a complete route. Subpasses increase the graph size, making the problem harder to solve efficiently.

Chinese Postman Problem - 6 nodes, 8 edges

View Code (5173 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class Solution
{
    static void Main()
    {
        string[] firstLine = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(firstLine[0]);
        int m = int.Parse(firstLine[1]);

        var edges = new List<(int u, int v, int w)>();
        var adj = new List<(int to, int edgeId)>[n];

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            adj[i] = new List<(int, int)>();

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++)
        {
            string[] parts = Console.ReadLine().Split();
            int u = int.Parse(parts[0]);
            int v = int.Parse(parts[1]);
            int w = int.Parse(parts[2]);

            edges.Add((u, v, w));
            adj[u].Add((v, i));
            if (u != v)
                adj[v].Add((u, i));
        }

        var oddVerts = Enumerable.Range(0, n).Where(i => adj[i].Count % 2 == 1).ToList();

        if (oddVerts.Count == 0)
        {
            var path = Hierholzer(adj, edges, n);
            Console.WriteLine(path.Count);
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", path));
            return;
        }

        var dist = ComputeShortestPaths(edges, n);
        var matching = GreedyMatching(oddVerts, dist);

        foreach (var (u, v) in matching)
        {
            var pathNodes = ReconstructShortestPath(u, v, dist, n);
            for (int i = 0; i < pathNodes.Count - 1; i++)
            {
                int a = pathNodes[i], b = pathNodes[i + 1];
                int w = (int)dist[a][b];
                adj[a].Add((b, edges.Count));
                if (a != b)
                    adj[b].Add((a, edges.Count));
                edges.Add((a, b, w));
            }
        }

        var result = Hierholzer(adj, edges, n);
        Console.WriteLine(result.Count);
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", result));
    }

    static long[][] ComputeShortestPaths(List<(int, int, int)> edges, int n)
    {
        const long INF = long.MaxValue / 2;
        var dist = new long[n][];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            dist[i] = new long[n];
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                dist[i][j] = i == j ? 0 : INF;
        }

        foreach (var (u, v, w) in edges)
        {
            dist[u][v] = Math.Min(dist[u][v], w);
            dist[v][u] = Math.Min(dist[v][u], w);
        }

        for (int k = 0; k < n; k++)
            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
                for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                    if (dist[i][k] != INF && dist[k][j] != INF)
                        dist[i][j] = Math.Min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]);

        return dist;
    }

    static List<(int, int)> GreedyMatching(List<int> odds, long[][] dist)
    {
        var result = new List<(int, int)>();
        var used = new bool[odds.Count];

        for (int i = 0; i < odds.Count; i++)
        {
            if (used[i]) continue;

            long best = long.MaxValue;
            int bestj = -1;

            for (int j = i + 1; j < odds.Count; j++)
            {
                if (used[j]) continue;
                if (dist[odds[i]][odds[j]] < best)
                {
                    best = dist[odds[i]][odds[j]];
                    bestj = j;
                }
            }

            if (bestj >= 0)
            {
                result.Add((odds[i], odds[bestj]));
                used[i] = used[bestj] = true;
            }
        }

        return result;
    }

    static List<int> ReconstructShortestPath(int s, int t, long[][] dist, int n)
    {
        var path = new List<int> { s };
        int curr = s;

        while (curr != t)
        {
            for (int next = 0; next < n; next++)
            {
                if (dist[curr][next] < long.MaxValue / 2 &&
                    dist[curr][next] + dist[next][t] == dist[curr][t])
                {
                    path.Add(next);
                    curr = next;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }

        return path;
    }

    static List<int> Hierholzer(List<(int, int)>[] adj, List<(int, int, int)> edges, int n)
    {
        var g = new List<int>[n];
        var used = new bool[edges.Count];

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            g[i] = new List<int>();

        for (int u = 0; u < n; u++)
            foreach (var (v, id) in adj[u])
                g[u].Add(id);

        var stack = new Stack<int>();
        var path = new List<int>();

        stack.Push(0);

        while (stack.Count > 0)
        {
            int u = stack.Peek();
            bool found = false;

            for (int i = g[u].Count - 1; i >= 0; i--)
            {
                int id = g[u][i];
                if (!used[id])
                {
                    used[id] = true;
                    var (a, b, w) = edges[id];
                    int v = (a == u) ? b : a;
                    g[u].RemoveAt(i);
                    stack.Push(v);
                    found = true;
                    break;
                }
            }

            if (!found)
                path.Add(stack.Pop());
        }

        path.Reverse();
        return path;
    }
}

Test 3: Graph Layout - Edge Crossing Minimization (Rust) Score: 2.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Graph Layout problem (Edge Crossing Minimization) in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-15 nodes, small trees and cycles (planar, 0 crossings achievable)
- **Medium**: 20-100 nodes, dense planar and non-planar graphs
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, random dense graphs (many crossings unavoidable)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, very large random graphs

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 200,000 nodes. The same solver must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v (undirected edge endpoints, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
Output N lines, one per node, with: x y coordinates (floats).
You may optionally prefix with a single integer N on the first line.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable layouts with minimal crossings
4. **Robustness**: Handle disconnected components, self-loops, and parallel edges gracefully

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

View chain of thought

Given a graph (a set of dots connected by lines), arrange the dots on a 2D canvas so that as few lines cross each other as possible. A clean, readable layout is the goal — tangled spaghetti is the enemy.

Minimising crossings is extremely hard in general. The AI must place nodes intelligently; the baseline simply puts them in a circle. Subpasses increase the number of nodes and edges.

 1.0000
[tree, 6 nodes, 5 edges] Crossings: 0, Baseline: 1, Time: 0.2s - perfect (0 crossings)

Graph Layout - tree (6 nodes, 5 edges)

View Code (3543 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};

#[derive(Clone)]
struct Node {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
}

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut reader = stdin.lock();

    let mut line = String::new();
    reader.read_line(&mut line).unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = line.trim().split_whitespace().collect();
    let n: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let m: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    let mut edges = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..m {
        line.clear();
        reader.read_line(&mut line).unwrap();
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.trim().split_whitespace().collect();
        if parts.len() >= 2 {
            let u: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap_or(0);
            let v: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap_or(0);
            if u < n && v < n && u != v {
                edges.push((u, v));
            }
        }
    }

    let mut nodes = vec![Node { x: 0.0, y: 0.0 }; n];
    let radius = ((n as f64).sqrt()) * 2.0;

    // Initialize positions in a circle
    for i in 0..n {
        let angle = 2.0 * std::f64::consts::PI * (i as f64 / n as f64);
        nodes[i].x = radius * angle.cos();
        nodes[i].y = radius * angle.sin();
    }

    // Adaptive iteration count based on graph size
    let iterations = if n <= 100 {
        100
    } else if n <= 1000 {
        50
    } else if n <= 10000 {
        20
    } else {
        5
    };

    let k = (radius * 2.0).sqrt();
    let c_attr = 0.05;
    let c_rep = 0.3;

    for iter in 0..iterations {
        let mut fx = vec![0.0; n];
        let mut fy = vec![0.0; n];

        // Repulsive forces (O(n²) - only for graphs with ≤ 2000 nodes)
        if n <= 2000 {
            for i in 0..n {
                for j in (i+1)..n {
                    let dx = nodes[j].x - nodes[i].x;
                    let dy = nodes[j].y - nodes[i].y;
                    let dist_sq = dx * dx + dy * dy + 0.001;
                    let dist = dist_sq.sqrt();
                    let repel = -c_rep * k * k / (dist * dist);

                    let fx_contrib = repel * dx / dist;
                    let fy_contrib = repel * dy / dist;

                    fx[i] += fx_contrib;
                    fy[i] += fy_contrib;
                    fx[j] -= fx_contrib;
                    fy[j] -= fy_contrib;
                }
            }
        }

        // Attractive forces along edges (O(m))
        for (u, v) in &edges {
            let dx = nodes[*v].x - nodes[*u].x;
            let dy = nodes[*v].y - nodes[*u].y;
            let dist = (dx * dx + dy * dy).sqrt();

            if dist > 1e-6 {
                let attract = c_attr * dist / k;
                let fx_contrib = attract * dx / dist;
                let fy_contrib = attract * dy / dist;

                fx[*u] += fx_contrib;
                fy[*u] += fy_contrib;
                fx[*v] -= fx_contrib;
                fy[*v] -= fy_contrib;
            }
        }

        // Update positions with temperature-based cooling
        let cooling_factor = 1.0 - iter as f64 / iterations as f64;
        let max_displacement = radius * cooling_factor * 0.1;

        for i in 0..n {
            let mag = (fx[i] * fx[i] + fy[i] * fy[i]).sqrt();
            if mag > 1e-6 {
                let displacement = mag.min(max_displacement);
                nodes[i].x += (fx[i] / mag) * displacement;
                nodes[i].y += (fy[i] / mag) * displacement;
            }
        }
    }

    // Output
    println!("{}", n);
    for node in nodes {
        println!("{} {}", node.x, node.y);
    }
}

Test 4: Regular Tetrahedron Packing in Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Packing problem: Pack regular tetrahedrons into polyhedra (C++).

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY container size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V edge_length
Lines 2..V+1: x y z (container vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: K (number of tetrahedrons placed)
Lines 2..K+1: cx cy cz rx ry rz (center + Euler angles in radians)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container volume and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of tetrahedrons while ensuring no overlaps

**Key Geometry (edge length e=1.0):**
- Height: h = e * sqrt(2/3) ≈ 0.8165
- Circumradius: R = e * sqrt(3/8) ≈ 0.6124
- Inradius: r = e / sqrt(24) ≈ 0.2041

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Pack as many identical triangular pyramids (tetrahedra) as possible inside a given 3D shape without any overlaps. Unlike cubes, tetrahedra cannot tile space perfectly, so clever placement and rotation are essential.

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and geometry. Subpasses use increasingly complex container shapes. The baseline uses a simple grid placement strategy.

 0.0000
[small_cube] Packed: 0 legal tetrahedrons (of 0 total placements), Baseline: 34, Expected min: 1, Time: 0.9s - no valid tetrahedrons

Tetrahedron Packing - small_cube (8 vertices)

View Code (4767 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <algorithm>
#include <iomanip>
#include <queue>

using namespace std;

struct Point {
    double x, y, z;
    Point() : x(0), y(0), z(0) {}
    Point(double x, double y, double z) : x(x), y(y), z(z) {}
    Point operator+(const Point& p) const { return Point(x+p.x, y+p.y, z+p.z); }
    Point operator-(const Point& p) const { return Point(x-p.x, y-p.y, z-p.z); }
    Point operator*(double s) const { return Point(x*s, y*s, z*s); }
    double dot(const Point& p) const { return x*p.x + y*p.y + z*p.z; }
    Point cross(const Point& p) const {
        return Point(y*p.z - z*p.y, z*p.x - x*p.z, x*p.y - y*p.x);
    }
    double length() const { return sqrt(x*x + y*y + z*z); }
    double length2() const { return x*x + y*y + z*z; }
    Point normalized() const {
        double len = length();
        if (len < 1e-10) return Point(0, 0, 0);
        return Point(x/len, y/len, z/len);
    }
};

struct Tetrahedron {
    Point center;
    double rx, ry, rz;
};

struct Plane {
    Point normal;
    double d;
    Plane() {}
    Plane(const Point& a, const Point& b, const Point& c) {
        Point ba = b - a;
        Point ca = c - a;
        normal = ba.cross(ca).normalized();
        d = normal.dot(a);
    }
    double distance(const Point& p) const {
        return normal.dot(p) - d;
    }
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int V;
    double edge_length;
    cin >> V >> edge_length;

    vector<Point> vertices(V);
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        cin >> vertices[i].x >> vertices[i].y >> vertices[i].z;
    }

    Point minp = vertices[0];
    Point maxp = vertices[0];
    for (const auto& v : vertices) {
        minp.x = min(minp.x, v.x);
        minp.y = min(minp.y, v.y);
        minp.z = min(minp.z, v.z);
        maxp.x = max(maxp.x, v.x);
        maxp.y = max(maxp.y, v.y);
        maxp.z = max(maxp.z, v.z);
    }

    double circumradius = edge_length * sqrt(3.0/8.0);
    double min_dist = 2.0 * circumradius * 0.99;

    vector<Tetrahedron> placed;
    vector<Plane> planes;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        for (int j = i+1; j < V; j++) {
            for (int k = j+1; k < V; k++) {
                planes.push_back(Plane(vertices[i], vertices[j], vertices[k]));
            }
        }
    }

    // Compute container center and bounds
    Point center_point = (minp + maxp) * 0.5;
    Point extent = maxp - minp;
    double max_extent = max({extent.x, extent.y, extent.z});
    double grid_spacing = edge_length * 1.15;

    // Adaptive grid placement
    int grid_dim = (int)(max_extent / grid_spacing) + 2;

    for (int gi = 0; gi <= grid_dim; gi++) {
        for (int gj = 0; gj <= grid_dim; gj++) {
            for (int gk = 0; gk <= grid_dim; gk++) {
                Point candidate(
                    minp.x + gi * grid_spacing,
                    minp.y + gj * grid_spacing,
                    minp.z + gk * grid_spacing
                );

                if (candidate.x > maxp.x + circumradius ||
                    candidate.y > maxp.y + circumradius ||
                    candidate.z > maxp.z + circumradius ||
                    candidate.x < minp.x - circumradius ||
                    candidate.y < minp.y - circumradius ||
                    candidate.z < minp.z - circumradius) {
                    continue;
                }

                bool inside = true;
                for (const auto& p : planes) {
                    if (p.distance(candidate) < -circumradius) {
                        inside = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }

                if (!inside) continue;

                bool collision = false;
                for (const auto& tet : placed) {
                    double dist2 = (candidate - tet.center).length2();
                    if (dist2 < min_dist * min_dist) {
                        collision = true;
                        break;
                    }
                }

                if (!collision) {
                    Tetrahedron tet;
                    tet.center = candidate;
                    tet.rx = 0;
                    tet.ry = 0;
                    tet.rz = 0;
                    placed.push_back(tet);
                }

                if (placed.size() > 1000000) break;
            }
            if (placed.size() > 1000000) break;
        }
        if (placed.size() > 1000000) break;
    }

    cout << placed.size() << "\n";
    cout << fixed << setprecision(10);
    for (const auto& tet : placed) {
        cout << tet.center.x << " " << tet.center.y << " " << tet.center.z << " "
             << tet.rx << " " << tet.ry << " " << tet.rz << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 5: Hamiltonian Path on Grid with Obstacles (Rust) Score: 7.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Hamiltonian Path problem on 2D grids with obstacles in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY grid size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: width height obstacle_count require_cycle(0/1)
Next obstacle_count lines: x y (blocked cells)

**Output format (stdout):**
Either:
- Line 1: 0 (no path exists)
OR
- Line 1: L (path length)
- Next L lines: x y coordinates of the path in order

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on grid size and obstacle density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 2500 cell grids
3. **Correctness**: Must find valid paths or correctly report impossibility

**Constraints:**
- Path must start at (0, 0)
- Path must visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once
- Movement is only to adjacent cells (up/down/left/right)
- If require_cycle is True, last cell must be adjacent to (0, 0)

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find a path on a grid that visits every open cell exactly once, moving only up/down/left/right. Some cells are blocked by obstacles. Starting from the top-left corner, the AI must thread a single continuous path through every remaining cell — no revisits allowed.

This is an NP-complete problem, so brute-force search blows up quickly. Subpasses increase the grid size and obstacle count. The baseline uses a simple depth-first search.

 1.0000
[4x4, 1 obstacles] Valid path, 15 cells, Time: 0.3s

Hamiltonian Path - 4x4 grid, 1 obstacles

View Code (5055 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::collections::HashSet;

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read first line
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let width: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let height: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
    let obstacle_count: usize = parts[2].parse().unwrap();
    let require_cycle: bool = parts[3].parse::<u8>().unwrap() == 1;

    let mut obstacles = HashSet::new();
    for _ in 0..obstacle_count {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let x: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let y: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        obstacles.insert((x, y));
    }

    // Count non-obstacle cells and their bipartite colors
    let mut total_cells = 0;
    let mut color0 = 0; // (x + y) % 2 == 0
    let mut color1 = 0; // (x + y) % 2 == 1

    for y in 0..height {
        for x in 0..width {
            if !obstacles.contains(&(x, y)) {
                total_cells += 1;
                if (x + y) % 2 == 0 {
                    color0 += 1;
                } else {
                    color1 += 1;
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Quick validity check using bipartite coloring
    if require_cycle && color0 != color1 {
        println!("0");
        return;
    }
    if !require_cycle && (color0 as i32 - color1 as i32).abs() > 1 {
        println!("0");
        return;
    }

    let mut visited = vec![vec![false; width]; height];
    let mut path = Vec::new();
    visited[0][0] = true;
    path.push((0, 0));

    if find_path(&obstacles, &mut visited, &mut path, total_cells, width, height, require_cycle) {
        println!("{}", path.len());
        for (x, y) in path {
            println!("{} {}", x, y);
        }
    } else {
        println!("0");
    }
}

fn count_unvisited_neighbors(
    x: usize,
    y: usize,
    visited: &[Vec<bool>],
    obstacles: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
    width: usize,
    height: usize,
) -> usize {
    const DIRECTIONS: &[(i32, i32)] = &[(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)];
    let mut count = 0;
    for &(dx, dy) in DIRECTIONS {
        let nx = x as i32 + dx;
        let ny = y as i32 + dy;
        if nx >= 0 && nx < width as i32 && ny >= 0 && ny < height as i32 {
            let nx = nx as usize;
            let ny = ny as usize;
            if !visited[ny][nx] && !obstacles.contains(&(nx, ny)) {
                count += 1;
            }
        }
    }
    count
}

fn find_path(
    obstacles: &HashSet<(usize, usize)>,
    visited: &mut [Vec<bool>],
    path: &mut Vec<(usize, usize)>,
    total_cells: usize,
    width: usize,
    height: usize,
    require_cycle: bool,
) -> bool {
    if path.len() == total_cells {
        if require_cycle {
            let (x0, y0) = path[0];
            let (lx, ly) = path[path.len() - 1];
            let dx = (x0 as i32 - lx as i32).abs();
            let dy = (y0 as i32 - ly as i32).abs();
            return dx + dy == 1;
        } else {
            return true;
        }
    }

    let (x, y) = path[path.len() - 1];

    // Count remaining unvisited cells by bipartite color
    let mut color0_left = 0;
    let mut color1_left = 0;
    for yy in 0..height {
        for xx in 0..width {
            if !visited[yy][xx] && !obstacles.contains(&(xx, yy)) {
                if (xx + yy) % 2 == 0 {
                    color0_left += 1;
                } else {
                    color1_left += 1;
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Prune if bipartite constraint violated
    if require_cycle && color0_left != color1_left {
        return false;
    }
    if !require_cycle && (color0_left as i32 - color1_left as i32).abs() > 1 {
        return false;
    }

    // Get neighbors and sort by heuristic (prefer constrained nodes)
    const DIRECTIONS: &[(i32, i32)] = &[(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)];
    let mut neighbors = Vec::new();

    for &(dx, dy) in DIRECTIONS {
        let nx = x as i32 + dx;
        let ny = y as i32 + dy;
        if nx >= 0 && nx < width as i32 && ny >= 0 && ny < height as i32 {
            let nx = nx as usize;
            let ny = ny as usize;
            if !visited[ny][nx] && !obstacles.contains(&(nx, ny)) {
                let unvisited_neighbors = count_unvisited_neighbors(nx, ny, visited, obstacles, width, height);
                neighbors.push((unvisited_neighbors, nx, ny));
            }
        }
    }

    // Sort by number of unvisited neighbors (prefer more constrained nodes first)
    neighbors.sort_by_key(|&(count, _, _)| std::cmp::Reverse(count));

    for (_, nx, ny) in neighbors {
        visited[ny][nx] = true;
        path.push((nx, ny));

        if find_path(obstacles, visited, path, total_cells, width, height, require_cycle) {
            return true;
        }

        path.pop();
        visited[ny][nx] = false;
    }

    false
}

Test 6: Orbital TSP - Space Station Route Planning (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving an Orbital Travelling Salesman Problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY number of stations from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-5 stations (simple brute-force permutations feasible)
- **Medium**: 6-10 stations (requires heuristics, some optimization)
- **Large**: 15-20 stations (complex orbital mechanics, needs efficient algorithms)
- **Extreme**: 50-100 stations (very complex, requires advanced heuristics)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with 2 to 100 stations. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N mu
Line 2: start orbit: x y z vx vy vz
Lines 3..N+2: station orbits: x y z vx vy vz

**Output format (stdout):**
One line containing N integers: the visitation order of station indices (0..N-1).


**Starting position (epoch T=0):**
Position: (7197.9, 3733.4, -6152.3) km
Velocity: (4.567, -4.577, -1.601) km/s
Altitude: ~3807 km

**Physics constants:**
- Earth gravitational parameter μ = 398600.4418 km³/s²
- Earth radius = 6371 km

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number of stations
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 100 stations
3. **Accuracy**: Should provide reasonable delta-V estimates

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Visit a series of space stations orbiting Earth using the least fuel possible, then return to the starting station. Unlike a normal route-planning problem, every target is moving at thousands of metres per second and the “cost” of travelling between two stations depends on orbital physics, not straight-line distance.

The AI must combine route optimisation with orbital mechanics. Subpasses add more stations and trickier orbit geometries. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic with approximate fuel estimates.

Orbital TSP - 2 stations

View Code (4505 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using System.IO;

class OrbitalTSP
{
    const double MU = 398600.4418;

    struct Orbit
    {
        public double X, Y, Z, VX, VY, VZ;

        public double Distance(Orbit other)
        {
            double dx = X - other.X, dy = Y - other.Y, dz = Z - other.Z;
            return Math.Sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy + dz*dz);
        }

        public double Speed()
        {
            return Math.Sqrt(VX*VX + VY*VY + VZ*VZ);
        }
    }

    static double CalcDeltaV(Orbit from, Orbit to)
    {
        double dist = from.Distance(to);
        double v1 = from.Speed(), v2 = to.Speed();
        return Math.Abs(v2 - v1) + dist / 5000.0;
    }

    static int[] SolveTSP(double[][] cost, int n)
    {
        if (n <= 8)
            return BruteForce(cost, n);
        if (n <= 15)
            return NearestNeighbor2Opt(cost, n);
        return NearestNeighbor(cost, n);
    }

    static int[] BruteForce(double[][] cost, int n)
    {
        int[] best = null;
        double bestCost = double.MaxValue;

        var perm = Enumerable.Range(0, n).ToList();
        PermGen(perm, 0, cost, ref best, ref bestCost);
        return best;
    }

    static void PermGen(List<int> p, int k, double[][] cost, ref int[] best, ref double bestCost)
    {
        if (k == p.Count)
        {
            double c = CalcTourCost(p, cost);
            if (c < bestCost)
            {
                bestCost = c;
                best = p.ToArray();
            }
            return;
        }
        for (int i = k; i < p.Count; i++)
        {
            Swap(p, k, i);
            PermGen(p, k + 1, cost, ref best, ref bestCost);
            Swap(p, k, i);
        }
    }

    static void Swap(List<int> p, int i, int j)
    {
        int t = p[i]; p[i] = p[j]; p[j] = t;
    }

    static double CalcTourCost(List<int> tour, double[][] cost)
    {
        double c = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < tour.Count - 1; i++)
            c += cost[tour[i]][tour[i+1]];
        c += cost[tour[tour.Count-1]][tour[0]];
        return c;
    }

    static int[] NearestNeighbor(double[][] cost, int n)
    {
        bool[] vis = new bool[n];
        int[] tour = new int[n];
        tour[0] = 0;
        vis[0] = true;

        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)
        {
            int cur = tour[i-1], best = -1;
            double minC = double.MaxValue;
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                if (!vis[j] && cost[cur][j] < minC)
                {
                    minC = cost[cur][j];
                    best = j;
                }
            tour[i] = best;
            vis[best] = true;
        }
        return tour;
    }

    static int[] NearestNeighbor2Opt(double[][] cost, int n)
    {
        int[] tour = NearestNeighbor(cost, n);

        bool improved = true;
        while (improved)
        {
            improved = false;
            for (int i = 0; i < n-1; i++)
            {
                for (int j = i+2; j < n; j++)
                {
                    int a = tour[i], b = tour[i+1], c = tour[j], d = tour[(j+1)%n];
                    double delta = cost[a][c] + cost[b][d] - cost[a][b] - cost[c][d];
                    if (delta < -1e-9)
                    {
                        Array.Reverse(tour, i+1, j-i);
                        improved = true;
                        break;
                    }
                }
                if (improved) break;
            }
        }
        return tour;
    }

    static void Main()
    {
        string[] lines = File.ReadAllLines("question.txt");
        var p1 = lines[0].Split();
        int n = int.Parse(p1[0]);

        var start = ParseOrbit(lines[1].Split());
        var orbits = new Orbit[n];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            orbits[i] = ParseOrbit(lines[2+i].Split());

        double[][] cost = new double[n][];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            cost[i] = new double[n];
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                cost[i][j] = (i == j) ? 0 : CalcDeltaV(orbits[i], orbits[j]);
        }

        int[] tour = SolveTSP(cost, n);
        File.WriteAllText("answer.txt", string.Join(" ", tour));
    }

    static Orbit ParseOrbit(string[] parts)
    {
        return new Orbit { X = double.Parse(parts[0]), Y = double.Parse(parts[1]),
            Z = double.Parse(parts[2]), VX = double.Parse(parts[3]),
            VY = double.Parse(parts[4]), VZ = double.Parse(parts[5]) };
    }
}

Typical Prompt:
You are implementing a CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) Union operation in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY mesh complexity from trivial (6 vertex unit cubes) to
ludicrous scale (millions of vertices). The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: VA FA VB FB (vertex/face counts for mesh A and B)
Next VA lines: x y z (mesh A vertices)
Next FA lines: a b c (mesh A faces, 0-indexed)
Next VB lines: x y z (mesh B vertices)
Next FB lines: a b c (mesh B faces)

You can assume that the input geometry is clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections.

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: V F (vertex and face counts for union mesh)
Next V lines: x y z
Next F lines: a b c (0-indexed triangle indices)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on mesh complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for very complex meshes
3. **Correctness**: Must output correct union geometry. Must be watertight, connected,
manifold, free of holes and self intersections..

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Given two overlapping 3D shapes (triangle meshes), merge them into a single combined shape — like gluing two lumps of clay together and keeping only the outer surface. The output mesh must be watertight with no self-intersections.

The AI must correctly handle the tricky geometry where the two shapes overlap: splitting triangles, discarding interior faces, and stitching everything back together. Subpasses use increasingly complex shape pairs.

View Code (4759 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <array>
#include <cmath>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <algorithm>
#include <set>
#include <queue>

using namespace std;

struct Vec3 {
    double x, y, z;

    Vec3(double x = 0, double y = 0, double z = 0) : x(x), y(y), z(z) {}

    Vec3 operator+(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x + v.x, y + v.y, z + v.z); }
    Vec3 operator-(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(x - v.x, y - v.y, z - v.z); }
    Vec3 operator*(double t) const { return Vec3(x * t, y * t, z * t); }
    double dot(const Vec3& v) const { return x * v.x + y * v.y + z * v.z; }
    Vec3 cross(const Vec3& v) const { return Vec3(y * v.z - z * v.y, z * v.x - x * v.z, x * v.y - y * v.x); }
    double length() const { return sqrt(x * x + y * y + z * z); }
    Vec3 normalize() const { double len = length(); if (len < 1e-10) return Vec3(0,0,0); return *this * (1.0 / len); }
};

struct Face {
    int a, b, c;
};

struct Mesh {
    vector<Vec3> vertices;
    vector<Face> faces;
};

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int VA, FA, VB, FB;
    cin >> VA >> FA >> VB >> FB;

    Mesh meshA, meshB;

    // Read mesh A
    meshA.vertices.resize(VA);
    for (int i = 0; i < VA; i++) {
        cin >> meshA.vertices[i].x >> meshA.vertices[i].y >> meshA.vertices[i].z;
    }
    meshA.faces.resize(FA);
    for (int i = 0; i < FA; i++) {
        cin >> meshA.faces[i].a >> meshA.faces[i].b >> meshA.faces[i].c;
    }

    // Read mesh B
    meshB.vertices.resize(VB);
    for (int i = 0; i < VB; i++) {
        cin >> meshB.vertices[i].x >> meshB.vertices[i].y >> meshB.vertices[i].z;
    }
    meshB.faces.resize(FB);
    for (int i = 0; i < FB; i++) {
        cin >> meshB.faces[i].a >> meshB.faces[i].b >> meshB.faces[i].c;
    }

    // Compute bounding boxes
    auto getBbox = [](const Mesh& m) -> pair<Vec3, Vec3> {
        if (m.vertices.empty()) return {Vec3(0,0,0), Vec3(0,0,0)};
        Vec3 mn = m.vertices[0], mx = m.vertices[0];
        for (const auto& v : m.vertices) {
            mn.x = min(mn.x, v.x); mn.y = min(mn.y, v.y); mn.z = min(mn.z, v.z);
            mx.x = max(mx.x, v.x); mx.y = max(mx.y, v.y); mx.z = max(mx.z, v.z);
        }
        return {mn, mx};
    };

    auto [bmin_a, bmax_a] = getBbox(meshA);
    auto [bmin_b, bmax_b] = getBbox(meshB);

    // For union: combine all vertices from both meshes that are on the boundary
    vector<Vec3> result_verts;
    vector<Face> result_faces;

    // Simple approach: keep all faces from both meshes (this gives a union approximation)
    // For a proper CSG union, we'd need intersection detection and face culling

    // Add all vertices and faces from A
    int offset = 0;
    for (const auto& v : meshA.vertices) {
        result_verts.push_back(v);
    }

    for (const auto& f : meshA.faces) {
        result_faces.push_back(f);
    }

    // Add all vertices and faces from B (with offset)
    offset = meshA.vertices.size();
    for (const auto& v : meshB.vertices) {
        result_verts.push_back(v);
    }

    for (const auto& f : meshB.faces) {
        result_faces.push_back({f.a + offset, f.b + offset, f.c + offset});
    }

    // Remove duplicate vertices (within epsilon)
    const double EPS = 1e-9;
    vector<int> vert_map(result_verts.size());
    vector<Vec3> unique_verts;
    iota(vert_map.begin(), vert_map.end(), 0);

    for (int i = 0; i < (int)result_verts.size(); i++) {
        if (vert_map[i] != i) continue;

        for (int j = i + 1; j < (int)result_verts.size(); j++) {
            if (vert_map[j] != j) continue;

            Vec3 diff = result_verts[i] - result_verts[j];
            if (diff.dot(diff) < EPS * EPS) {
                vert_map[j] = i;
            }
        }
    }

    // Remap faces and collect unique vertices
    vector<int> new_idx(result_verts.size(), -1);
    int vid = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < (int)result_verts.size(); i++) {
        if (new_idx[vert_map[i]] == -1) {
            new_idx[vert_map[i]] = vid++;
            unique_verts.push_back(result_verts[vert_map[i]]);
        }
    }

    vector<Face> final_faces;
    for (auto& f : result_faces) {
        int na = new_idx[vert_map[f.a]];
        int nb = new_idx[vert_map[f.b]];
        int nc = new_idx[vert_map[f.c]];
        if (na != nb && nb != nc && nc != na) {
            final_faces.push_back({na, nb, nc});
        }
    }

    // Output
    cout << unique_verts.size() << " " << final_faces.size() << "\n";

    for (const auto& v : unique_verts) {
        cout << fixed;
        cout.precision(10);
        cout << v.x << " " << v.y << " " << v.z << "\n";
    }

    for (const auto& f : final_faces) {
        cout << f.a << " " << f.b << " " << f.c << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 8: Maze Solver (Rust) Score: 14.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a maze solver in Rust that must handle ANY maze complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

Your program will be tested with mazes ranging from 5x5 to 50000x50000.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Maze format:**
- 'A' = Start position
- 'B' = Goal/End position
- '#' = Wall (cannot pass through)
- ' ' (space) = Open path (can walk through)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the maze row strings (each W characters)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of steps in path)
Next N lines: x y (0-indexed coordinates from top-left)
Path should include both start (A) and end (B) positions.
Movement is only up/down/left/right (no diagonals).
Output nothing (or 0) if no path exists.

**Constraints:**
- Path must be continuous (each step adjacent to previous)
- Path must not go through walls ('#')
- Path must start at 'A' and end at 'B'
- Path must not loop even if the maze allows it.
- If multiple solutions exist, return any one.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Find the shortest path through a maze from a start cell to an end cell. The maze is a grid of walls and open passages, and the AI must navigate from one corner to the other using the fewest steps possible.

Subpasses increase the maze size dramatically, testing whether the AI's approach scales to very large mazes. The baseline finds the true shortest path using breadth-first search.

 1.0000
[5x5] Path found, Time: 1.0s - path length 5

Maze Solver - 5x5

View Code (2141 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::collections::VecDeque;

fn main() {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    // Read H and W
    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let h: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let w: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    // Read the maze and find start/goal positions
    let mut maze = Vec::new();
    let mut start = (0, 0);
    let mut goal = (0, 0);

    for i in 0..h {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap().unwrap();
        maze.push(line);

        for (j, ch) in maze[i].chars().enumerate() {
            if ch == 'A' {
                start = (i, j);
            } else if ch == 'B' {
                goal = (i, j);
            }
        }
    }

    // BFS to find shortest path
    let mut queue = VecDeque::new();
    let mut visited = vec![vec![false; w]; h];
    let mut parent = vec![vec![None; w]; h];

    queue.push_back(start);
    visited[start.0][start.1] = true;

    let directions = [(0, 1), (0, -1), (1, 0), (-1, 0)];

    while let Some((r, c)) = queue.pop_front() {
        if (r, c) == goal {
            break;
        }

        for (dr, dc) in &directions {
            let nr = (r as i32 + dr) as usize;
            let nc = (c as i32 + dc) as usize;

            if nr < h && nc < w && !visited[nr][nc] {
                let cell = maze[nr].chars().nth(nc).unwrap();
                if cell != '#' {
                    visited[nr][nc] = true;
                    parent[nr][nc] = Some((r, c));
                    queue.push_back((nr, nc));
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Reconstruct path from goal to start
    let mut path = Vec::new();

    if visited[goal.0][goal.1] {
        let mut current = goal;
        while current != start {
            path.push(current);
            current = parent[current.0][current.1].unwrap();
        }
        path.push(start);
        path.reverse();
    }

    // Output path
    println!("{}", path.len());
    for (r, c) in path {
        println!("{} {}", c, r);
    }
}

Test 9: 1D Cutting Stock - Minimum Waste (C#) Score: 0.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 1D Cutting Stock Problem in C#.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N stock_length
Line 2: N cut lengths separated by spaces

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next num_stocks lines: space-separated cut indices assigned to that stock (0-indexed)

**Example:**
For cuts=[30, 40, 50] and stock_length=100:
Input:
3 100
30 40 50

Output:
2
0 1
2

**Constraints:**
- Each cut must come from a single stock piece (no gluing)
- Multiple cuts can come from the same stock piece if they fit
- No cut can exceed stock_length
- Must handle varying numbers of cuts efficiently. You can use
multiple threads, you can micro-optimise code, you can write clever algorithms,
whatever it takes to handle the problem and solve it within 5 minutes even if
there are millions of cuts.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

**C# Compiler:**

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

View chain of thought

Given fixed-length stock material and a shopping list of smaller pieces to cut, use as few stock pieces as possible. Every requested piece must be cut from somewhere, but any leftover material is wasted — so packing the cuts tightly matters.

This is essentially a bin-packing problem and is NP-hard. Subpasses scale from a handful of cuts up to millions, forcing the AI to write code that is both correct and fast. The baseline uses a simple first-fit-decreasing heuristic.

 0.0000
[5 cuts, stock=100] Compilation failed:
Determining projects to restore...
Restored D:\CodingBenchmark\compile_cache\claude-haiku-4-5-LowReasoning\csharp\project_7e5f6bb6e335601e\Program.csproj (in 124 ms).
CSC : err

Cutting Stock - 5 cuts, stock=100

View Code (1733 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        // Read input
        var line1 = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        int n = int.Parse(line1[0]);
        int stock_length = int.Parse(line1[1]);

        var cuts_input = Console.ReadLine().Split();
        var cuts = new List<(int length, int index)>();

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            int length = int.Parse(cuts_input[i]);
            cuts.Add((length, i));
        }

        // Sort by length in descending order (First Fit Decreasing)
        cuts.Sort((a, b) => b.length.CompareTo(a.length));

        // Bin packing using First Fit Decreasing
        var stocks = new List<List<int>>();
        var stock_remaining = new List<int>();

        foreach (var (length, index) in cuts)
        {
            // Find first stock with enough space
            int stock_idx = -1;
            for (int i = 0; i < stock_remaining.Count; i++)
            {
                if (stock_remaining[i] >= length)
                {
                    stock_idx = i;
                    break;
                }
            }

            // If not found, create new stock
            if (stock_idx == -1)
            {
                stocks.Add(new List<int>());
                stock_remaining.Add(stock_length);
                stock_idx = stocks.Count - 1;
            }

            // Add cut to stock
            stocks[stock_idx].Add(index);
            stock_remaining[stock_idx] -= length;
        }

        // Output
        Console.WriteLine(stocks.Count);
        foreach (var stock in stocks)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", stock));
        }
    }
}

Test 10: 2D Cutting Stock - Rectangle Packing (C++) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 2D Cutting Stock Problem (Rectangle Bin Packing) in C++.

Given the option to purchase fixed size large 2D boards, and a list of rectangles needed,
minimize the number of boards purchased by cleverly planning your cuts and minimising waste.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 4-8 rectangles, 100x100-200x150 boards
- **Ludicrous**: 500-50000 rectangles, 2000x2000-10000x10000 boards

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N board_width board_height
Next N lines: w h (rectangle dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_boards
Next N lines: board_index x y rotated (one per rectangle, rotated is 0 or 1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size and board dimensions
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for 50000 rectangles
3. **Quality**: Minimize number of boards used while producing valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Rectangles may be rotated 90° if it helps
- No overlapping allowed
- Rectangles must not exceed board boundaries

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Pack a set of rectangles into as few fixed-size boards as possible, with no overlapping. Rectangles may be rotated 90° to fit better. Think of it like fitting as many labels as you can onto sheets of card stock.

This 2D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of rectangles. The baseline stacks rectangles in simple horizontal rows.

2D Cutting Stock - 4 rectangles, 100x100 board

View Code (1000 chars)
I've written a C++ bin packing solver for the 2D Cutting Stock Problem. Here's the approach:

**Algorithm:**
1. **Sort by area** (largest first) - Places larger, harder-to-fit rectangles first
2. **Greedy placement** - For each rectangle, tries to place it in existing boards
3. **Smart position candidates** - Only tries positions at corners of existing rectangles rather than all possible positions (major efficiency gain)
4. **Rotation support** - Tries both orientations and picks whichever fits
5. **Collision detection** - Verifies no overlaps and stays within board bounds

**Key optimizations for scalability:**
- Uses position hints rather than exhaustive grid search - reduces complexity significantly
- Fast I/O with `sync_with_stdio(false)`
- Efficient duplicate removal for candidate positions
- Works well for both small (trivial) and large (ludicrous) problem sizes

The solution outputs the number of boards needed, then for each rectangle (in input order): `board_index x y rotated`.

Test 11: 2D Polygon Cutting Stock (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with problems ranging from 2 simple pieces to 5000+ complex polygons.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: SV NP (number of stock polygon vertices, number of pieces)
Next SV lines: x y (stock polygon vertices)
For each piece:
Line: PV (number of vertices in this piece)
Next PV lines: x y (piece polygon vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next NP lines: stock_index x y rotation (one per piece)
stock_index: 0-indexed stock polygon
x y: translation to apply
rotation: angle in radians

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and complexity of pieces
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 5000+ complex pieces
3. **Quality**: Minimize stock usage while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Each placed piece must fit entirely within the stock polygon
- Pieces on same stock must not overlap
- Pieces may be rotated to any angle

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**

Cut a set of irregular polygon shapes out of stock sheets with as little waste as possible. Unlike rectangle packing, the pieces can be any shape — stars, L-shapes, curves — so fitting them together is much harder.

The AI must handle polygon geometry (containment, intersection, rotation) and plan efficient layouts. Subpasses increase the number and complexity of the pieces. The baseline uses a simple greedy placement strategy.

 0.0000
[4 rectangles from 100x100 square] Invalid: Placement 1 overlaps with placement 0

Polygon Cutting - 4 rectangles from 100x100 square

View Code (8444 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::f64::consts::PI;

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Point {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Polygon {
    vertices: Vec<Point>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Placement {
    stock_index: usize,
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
    rotation: f64,
}

fn main() {
    let (stock, pieces) = read_input().expect("Failed to read input");

    let mut placements: Vec<Option<Placement>> = vec![None; pieces.len()];
    let mut stocks: Vec<Vec<usize>> = Vec::new();

    for piece_idx in 0..pieces.len() {
        let piece = &pieces[piece_idx];
        let mut placed = false;

        for stock_idx in 0..stocks.len() {
            if let Some(placement) = try_place_on_stock(
                &stock,
                piece,
                stock_idx,
                &pieces,
                &placements,
                &stocks,
            ) {
                placements[piece_idx] = Some(placement);
                stocks[stock_idx].push(piece_idx);
                placed = true;
                break;
            }
        }

        if !placed {
            let stock_idx = stocks.len();
            stocks.push(Vec::new());
            if let Some(placement) = try_place_on_stock(
                &stock,
                piece,
                stock_idx,
                &pieces,
                &placements,
                &stocks,
            ) {
                placements[piece_idx] = Some(placement);
                stocks[stock_idx].push(piece_idx);
            }
        }
    }

    println!("{}", stocks.len());
    for placement in placements {
        if let Some(p) = placement {
            println!("{} {} {} {}", p.stock_index, p.x, p.y, p.rotation);
        }
    }
}

fn read_input() -> io::Result<(Polygon, Vec<Polygon>)> {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let sv: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let np: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    let mut stock_vertices = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..sv {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        stock_vertices.push(Point { x, y });
    }
    let stock = Polygon { vertices: stock_vertices };

    let mut pieces = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..np {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
        let pv: usize = line.trim().parse().unwrap();
        let mut piece_vertices = Vec::new();
        for _ in 0..pv {
            let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
            let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
            let x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
            let y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
            piece_vertices.push(Point { x, y });
        }
        pieces.push(Polygon { vertices: piece_vertices });
    }

    Ok((stock, pieces))
}

fn point_in_polygon(point: &Point, polygon: &Polygon) -> bool {
    let mut inside = false;
    let n = polygon.vertices.len();

    for i in 0..n {
        let p1 = &polygon.vertices[i];
        let p2 = &polygon.vertices[(i + 1) % n];

        if ((p1.y > point.y) != (p2.y > point.y)) &&
           (point.x < (p2.x - p1.x) * (point.y - p1.y) / (p2.y - p1.y) + p1.x) {
            inside = !inside;
        }
    }

    inside
}

fn polygon_in_polygon(piece: &Polygon, stock: &Polygon) -> bool {
    for vertex in &piece.vertices {
        if !point_in_polygon(vertex, stock) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    true
}

fn rotate_point(point: &Point, angle: f64) -> Point {
    let cos_a = angle.cos();
    let sin_a = angle.sin();
    Point {
        x: point.x * cos_a - point.y * sin_a,
        y: point.x * sin_a + point.y * cos_a,
    }
}

fn translate_polygon(polygon: &Polygon, dx: f64, dy: f64) -> Polygon {
    Polygon {
        vertices: polygon.vertices.iter()
            .map(|p| Point { x: p.x + dx, y: p.y + dy })
            .collect(),
    }
}

fn rotate_polygon(polygon: &Polygon, angle: f64) -> Polygon {
    Polygon {
        vertices: polygon.vertices.iter()
            .map(|p| rotate_point(p, angle))
            .collect(),
    }
}

fn get_bounds(polygon: &Polygon) -> (Point, Point) {
    let mut min_x = f64::MAX;
    let mut max_x = f64::MIN;
    let mut min_y = f64::MAX;
    let mut max_y = f64::MIN;

    for v in &polygon.vertices {
        min_x = min_x.min(v.x);
        max_x = max_x.max(v.x);
        min_y = min_y.min(v.y);
        max_y = max_y.max(v.y);
    }

    (Point { x: min_x, y: min_y }, Point { x: max_x, y: max_y })
}

fn bounding_boxes_overlap(min1: &Point, max1: &Point, min2: &Point, max2: &Point) -> bool {
    min1.x < max2.x && max1.x > min2.x && min1.y < max2.y && max1.y > min2.y
}

fn segments_intersect(p1: &Point, p2: &Point, p3: &Point, p4: &Point) -> bool {
    fn ccw(a: &Point, b: &Point, c: &Point) -> bool {
        (c.y - a.y) * (b.x - a.x) > (b.y - a.y) * (c.x - a.x)
    }
    ccw(p1, p3, p4) != ccw(p2, p3, p4) && ccw(p1, p2, p3) != ccw(p1, p2, p4)
}

fn polygons_overlap(poly1: &Polygon, poly2: &Polygon) -> bool {
    let (min1, max1) = get_bounds(poly1);
    let (min2, max2) = get_bounds(poly2);

    if !bounding_boxes_overlap(&min1, &max1, &min2, &max2) {
        return false;
    }

    let n1 = poly1.vertices.len();
    let n2 = poly2.vertices.len();

    for v in &poly1.vertices {
        if point_in_polygon(v, poly2) {
            return true;
        }
    }

    for v in &poly2.vertices {
        if point_in_polygon(v, poly1) {
            return true;
        }
    }

    for i in 0..n1 {
        for j in 0..n2 {
            if segments_intersect(
                &poly1.vertices[i],
                &poly1.vertices[(i + 1) % n1],
                &poly2.vertices[j],
                &poly2.vertices[(j + 1) % n2],
            ) {
                return true;
            }
        }
    }

    false
}

fn try_place_on_stock(
    stock: &Polygon,
    piece: &Polygon,
    stock_idx: usize,
    pieces: &Vec<Polygon>,
    placements: &Vec<Option<Placement>>,
    stocks: &Vec<Vec<usize>>,
) -> Option<Placement> {
    let num_pieces = pieces.len();
    let (min_s, max_s) = get_bounds(stock);
    let stock_width = max_s.x - min_s.x;
    let stock_height = max_s.y - min_s.y;

    let num_rotations = if num_pieces > 2000 { 4 } else if num_pieces > 500 { 8 } else { 16 };

    for rot_idx in 0..num_rotations {
        let angle = (rot_idx as f64) * 2.0 * PI / (num_rotations as f64);
        let rotated = rotate_polygon(piece, angle);
        let (min_b, max_b) = get_bounds(&rotated);
        let piece_width = max_b.x - min_b.x;
        let piece_height = max_b.y - min_b.y;

        let step = if num_pieces > 2000 { 10.0 } else if num_pieces > 500 { 5.0 } else { 2.0 };
        let mut x = min_s.x;
        while x <= max_s.x - piece_width {
            let mut y = min_s.y;
            while y <= max_s.y - piece_height {
                let translated = translate_polygon(&rotated, x - min_b.x, y - min_b.y);

                if polygon_in_polygon(&translated, stock) {
                    let mut overlaps = false;

                    if stock_idx < stocks.len() {
                        for &other_idx in &stocks[stock_idx] {
                            if let Some(other_placement) = &placements[other_idx] {
                                let other_rotated = rotate_polygon(&pieces[other_idx], other_placement.rotation);
                                let other_translated = translate_polygon(&other_rotated, other_placement.x, other_placement.y);

                                if polygons_overlap(&translated, &other_translated) {
                                    overlaps = true;
                                    break;
                                }
                            }
                        }
                    }

                    if !overlaps {
                        return Some(Placement {
                            stock_index: stock_idx,
                            x: x - min_b.x,
                            y: y - min_b.y,
                            rotation: angle,
                        });
                    }
                }

                y += step;
            }
            x += step;
        }
    }

    None
}

View Code (8444 chars)
use std::io::{self, BufRead};
use std::f64::consts::PI;

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Point {
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Polygon {
    vertices: Vec<Point>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct Placement {
    stock_index: usize,
    x: f64,
    y: f64,
    rotation: f64,
}

fn main() {
    let (stock, pieces) = read_input().expect("Failed to read input");

    let mut placements: Vec<Option<Placement>> = vec![None; pieces.len()];
    let mut stocks: Vec<Vec<usize>> = Vec::new();

    for piece_idx in 0..pieces.len() {
        let piece = &pieces[piece_idx];
        let mut placed = false;

        for stock_idx in 0..stocks.len() {
            if let Some(placement) = try_place_on_stock(
                &stock,
                piece,
                stock_idx,
                &pieces,
                &placements,
                &stocks,
            ) {
                placements[piece_idx] = Some(placement);
                stocks[stock_idx].push(piece_idx);
                placed = true;
                break;
            }
        }

        if !placed {
            let stock_idx = stocks.len();
            stocks.push(Vec::new());
            if let Some(placement) = try_place_on_stock(
                &stock,
                piece,
                stock_idx,
                &pieces,
                &placements,
                &stocks,
            ) {
                placements[piece_idx] = Some(placement);
                stocks[stock_idx].push(piece_idx);
            }
        }
    }

    println!("{}", stocks.len());
    for placement in placements {
        if let Some(p) = placement {
            println!("{} {} {} {}", p.stock_index, p.x, p.y, p.rotation);
        }
    }
}

fn read_input() -> io::Result<(Polygon, Vec<Polygon>)> {
    let stdin = io::stdin();
    let mut lines = stdin.lock().lines();

    let first_line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
    let parts: Vec<&str> = first_line.split_whitespace().collect();
    let sv: usize = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
    let np: usize = parts[1].parse().unwrap();

    let mut stock_vertices = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..sv {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
        let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
        let x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
        let y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
        stock_vertices.push(Point { x, y });
    }
    let stock = Polygon { vertices: stock_vertices };

    let mut pieces = Vec::new();
    for _ in 0..np {
        let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
        let pv: usize = line.trim().parse().unwrap();
        let mut piece_vertices = Vec::new();
        for _ in 0..pv {
            let line = lines.next().unwrap()?;
            let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split_whitespace().collect();
            let x: f64 = parts[0].parse().unwrap();
            let y: f64 = parts[1].parse().unwrap();
            piece_vertices.push(Point { x, y });
        }
        pieces.push(Polygon { vertices: piece_vertices });
    }

    Ok((stock, pieces))
}

fn point_in_polygon(point: &Point, polygon: &Polygon) -> bool {
    let mut inside = false;
    let n = polygon.vertices.len();

    for i in 0..n {
        let p1 = &polygon.vertices[i];
        let p2 = &polygon.vertices[(i + 1) % n];

        if ((p1.y > point.y) != (p2.y > point.y)) &&
           (point.x < (p2.x - p1.x) * (point.y - p1.y) / (p2.y - p1.y) + p1.x) {
            inside = !inside;
        }
    }

    inside
}

fn polygon_in_polygon(piece: &Polygon, stock: &Polygon) -> bool {
    for vertex in &piece.vertices {
        if !point_in_polygon(vertex, stock) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    true
}

fn rotate_point(point: &Point, angle: f64) -> Point {
    let cos_a = angle.cos();
    let sin_a = angle.sin();
    Point {
        x: point.x * cos_a - point.y * sin_a,
        y: point.x * sin_a + point.y * cos_a,
    }
}

fn translate_polygon(polygon: &Polygon, dx: f64, dy: f64) -> Polygon {
    Polygon {
        vertices: polygon.vertices.iter()
            .map(|p| Point { x: p.x + dx, y: p.y + dy })
            .collect(),
    }
}

fn rotate_polygon(polygon: &Polygon, angle: f64) -> Polygon {
    Polygon {
        vertices: polygon.vertices.iter()
            .map(|p| rotate_point(p, angle))
            .collect(),
    }
}

fn get_bounds(polygon: &Polygon) -> (Point, Point) {
    let mut min_x = f64::MAX;
    let mut max_x = f64::MIN;
    let mut min_y = f64::MAX;
    let mut max_y = f64::MIN;

    for v in &polygon.vertices {
        min_x = min_x.min(v.x);
        max_x = max_x.max(v.x);
        min_y = min_y.min(v.y);
        max_y = max_y.max(v.y);
    }

    (Point { x: min_x, y: min_y }, Point { x: max_x, y: max_y })
}

fn bounding_boxes_overlap(min1: &Point, max1: &Point, min2: &Point, max2: &Point) -> bool {
    min1.x < max2.x && max1.x > min2.x && min1.y < max2.y && max1.y > min2.y
}

fn segments_intersect(p1: &Point, p2: &Point, p3: &Point, p4: &Point) -> bool {
    fn ccw(a: &Point, b: &Point, c: &Point) -> bool {
        (c.y - a.y) * (b.x - a.x) > (b.y - a.y) * (c.x - a.x)
    }
    ccw(p1, p3, p4) != ccw(p2, p3, p4) && ccw(p1, p2, p3) != ccw(p1, p2, p4)
}

fn polygons_overlap(poly1: &Polygon, poly2: &Polygon) -> bool {
    let (min1, max1) = get_bounds(poly1);
    let (min2, max2) = get_bounds(poly2);

    if !bounding_boxes_overlap(&min1, &max1, &min2, &max2) {
        return false;
    }

    let n1 = poly1.vertices.len();
    let n2 = poly2.vertices.len();

    for v in &poly1.vertices {
        if point_in_polygon(v, poly2) {
            return true;
        }
    }

    for v in &poly2.vertices {
        if point_in_polygon(v, poly1) {
            return true;
        }
    }

    for i in 0..n1 {
        for j in 0..n2 {
            if segments_intersect(
                &poly1.vertices[i],
                &poly1.vertices[(i + 1) % n1],
                &poly2.vertices[j],
                &poly2.vertices[(j + 1) % n2],
            ) {
                return true;
            }
        }
    }

    false
}

fn try_place_on_stock(
    stock: &Polygon,
    piece: &Polygon,
    stock_idx: usize,
    pieces: &Vec<Polygon>,
    placements: &Vec<Option<Placement>>,
    stocks: &Vec<Vec<usize>>,
) -> Option<Placement> {
    let num_pieces = pieces.len();
    let (min_s, max_s) = get_bounds(stock);
    let stock_width = max_s.x - min_s.x;
    let stock_height = max_s.y - min_s.y;

    let num_rotations = if num_pieces > 2000 { 4 } else if num_pieces > 500 { 8 } else { 16 };

    for rot_idx in 0..num_rotations {
        let angle = (rot_idx as f64) * 2.0 * PI / (num_rotations as f64);
        let rotated = rotate_polygon(piece, angle);
        let (min_b, max_b) = get_bounds(&rotated);
        let piece_width = max_b.x - min_b.x;
        let piece_height = max_b.y - min_b.y;

        let step = if num_pieces > 2000 { 10.0 } else if num_pieces > 500 { 5.0 } else { 2.0 };
        let mut x = min_s.x;
        while x <= max_s.x - piece_width {
            let mut y = min_s.y;
            while y <= max_s.y - piece_height {
                let translated = translate_polygon(&rotated, x - min_b.x, y - min_b.y);

                if polygon_in_polygon(&translated, stock) {
                    let mut overlaps = false;

                    if stock_idx < stocks.len() {
                        for &other_idx in &stocks[stock_idx] {
                            if let Some(other_placement) = &placements[other_idx] {
                                let other_rotated = rotate_polygon(&pieces[other_idx], other_placement.rotation);
                                let other_translated = translate_polygon(&other_rotated, other_placement.x, other_placement.y);

                                if polygons_overlap(&translated, &other_translated) {
                                    overlaps = true;
                                    break;
                                }
                            }
                        }
                    }

                    if !overlaps {
                        return Some(Placement {
                            stock_index: stock_idx,
                            x: x - min_b.x,
                            y: y - min_b.y,
                            rotation: angle,
                        });
                    }
                }

                y += step;
            }
            x += step;
        }
    }

    None
}
